title: Pre-Trained Language Models for Interactive Decision-Making

structured.summary: The paper explores using pre-trained language models (LMs) for sequential decision-making tasks, beyond traditional language processing. It introduces the LID framework, which utilizes LMs to improve policy generalization across different environments, even those without expert demonstrations, through an Active Data Gathering procedure.

strengths.evidence: The LID framework improved task completion rates by 43.6% in environments leveraging expert demonstrations and outperformed baselines by 25.1% in scenarios without them.
The paper shows that sequential input representations and favorable weight initialization significantly enhance model generalization.

weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks details on how embeddings are constructed and how the policy network architecture leverages them (lines 36-41).
The evaluation primarily focuses on the VirtualHome environment without testing across other diverse settings or using a wider range of baselines (lines 42-61).
The paper does not provide comprehensive details about experimental setups, hyperparameters, or model configurations (lines 123-123).

questions.question: How do sequential input representations improve decision-making policies?
questions.motivation: Understanding the underlying factors can help in replicating and further improving the model's performance and applicability.

critique_points.point_001: Limited clarity in methodology. The paper lacks details on how embeddings are constructed and how the policy network architecture leverages them (lines 36-41)
critique_points.point_002: Narrow evaluation scope. The evaluation primarily focuses on the VirtualHome environment without testing across other diverse settings or using a wider range of baselines (lines 42-61)
critique_points.point_003: Lack of reproducibility details. The paper does not provide comprehensive details about experimental setups, hyperparameters, or model configurations (lines 123-123)
